frameworks.name: Half-Step Frame Repackaging
frameworks.definition: Keep existing content substance (hooks, stories, topics) unchanged but shift the narrative frame from end-user perspective to practitioner-facing case-study perspective, changing the audience attracted without rebuilding the content library.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a large misaligned audience is worse than a small aligned one: a life coach trainer with 250k Instagram followers and $3M in annual revenue from certification programs had zero crossover between her audience (relationship-content followers) and her buyers (aspiring life coaches), illustrating that audience size confers no conversion advantage when content and offer target different people.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: baited the hook with the wrong bait
conditions: social media organic audience built around content topic that differs from the paid offer topic
claims.mechanism: Interest-based algorithm distribution means a large audience built around topic A will consist almost entirely of people interested in A, not B; size does not substitute for topic alignment when monetizing.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that because modern social platforms distribute content based on interest rather than social graph, a creator can pivot content topic and quickly attract a differently composed audience without being penned in by existing follower base; a piece of content about business shown to people interested in business will be distributed to business-interested users regardless of a creator's prior relationship-content follower count.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: interest-based media, not social media anymore
conditions: Instagram, TikTok, and equivalent interest-graph platforms
claims.mechanism: Modern platforms algorithmically distribute content to users by interest signal rather than to an account's existing follower list, so new content on a new topic reaches a new audience segment rather than the old one.
bounded_by: May not apply equally to platforms with strong social-graph distribution (e.g., early Facebook, LinkedIn organic)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that giving away the substance of a paid program in free content will not cannibalize sales, for two compounding reasons: most people who consume the free content will not implement it, and among those who do implement and get results, the success will increase their desire to buy more.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: your fear like what if i give away my secrets it will not matter
claims.mechanism: Non-implementation is the dominant behavioral outcome of free content consumption; those who do implement successfully self-select into wanting more depth, making them higher-probability buyers, not lost prospects.
bounded_by: Does not apply to commoditized software or products where the free version functionally replaces the paid version

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a creator or business owner whose content attracts the end-user of their clients (rather than the client themselves) is marketing perfectly for downstream customer acquisition while failing entirely at marketing for practitioner or partner acquisition; the same content strategy that maximizes one outcome minimizes the other.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: you're doing a perfect job for marketing for customers, not for business owners
conditions: B2B certification or training business where the seller's clients serve an end-consumer
claims.mechanism: Content framed from a consumer-facing perspective attracts consumers; content framed from a practitioner-facing perspective attracts practitioners; the framing determines which segment self-identifies with the content.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that reframing existing content from an end-user narrative to a practitioner case-study narrative (e.g., changing from 'here is how to heal your relationship trauma' to 'here is a client who came to me with this issue and this is how I handled it') shifts the audience attracted to aspiring practitioners while preserving the same hook mechanics and story structures that made the original content work.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: organic video content on social media
claims.mechanism: Audience self-selection is driven by the implied identity the content addresses; a story told as 'here is what happened to me' attracts people who share that identity, while 'here is what my client experienced and how I helped them' attracts people who want to be the helper.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that victim-framed content (content that portrays pain, trauma, and struggle from the sufferer's perspective) systematically attracts an audience that identifies as victims and wants to remain in that identity rather than take action to change; a pivot away from victim framing repels that segment and attracts more action-oriented audiences.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: victims will be attracted to a pro-victim narrative
claims.mechanism: Content narrative framing acts as an audience filter: audiences self-select based on whether the content's implied identity (victim or agent) matches their current self-perception; pro-victim framing continuously replenishes an audience of people who will not buy solutions.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi references a case where a founder he knew built a $50 million life-coaching business by combining two customer tiers — direct life-coaching clients alongside life-coach certification sales — and anchored both to a shared theme (money as a source of life problems), achieving content-offer alignment across both products simultaneously rather than choosing one audience.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: life-coaching or transformation business serving both end-consumers and aspiring practitioners
single unifying content theme (e.g., money) that naturally connects both audiences
claims.mechanism: A unifying theme that is relevant to both the practitioner audience and the end-consumer audience allows a single content stream to feed two offer tiers; without the unifying theme, two content streams fragment the brand.

numbers.axis: business revenue scale achieved with dual-tier life-coaching model

anchors.region: dual-tier life-coaching + certification model (unnamed founder, money-theme anchor)

bounded_by: Applies when there is a genuine thematic overlap between the end-consumer pain and the aspiring-practitioner desire; does not apply when the two audiences share no content theme

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a creator who is bored by or disengaged from a content topic will produce worse content on that topic, reducing the effectiveness of that content as a marketing channel, suggesting that content topic selection must account for the creator's genuine interest level, not only audience fit.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: organic long-form or short-form content creator
topics the creator finds uninteresting relative to alternatives
bounded_by: Does not apply to scripted or ghostwritten content where creator disengagement is not visible to audience

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for a creator already producing high-quality content in a domain, the effective pivot is typically a frame adjustment of roughly six inches rather than a full content strategy overhaul; the hooks, stories, and expertise remain intact while only the implied audience identity in the framing changes.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: you're probably like six inches off
conditions: established creator with existing content volume
case where offer and content topic are substantively related but the narrative frame targets the wrong audience segment
bounded_by: Does not apply when content topic is entirely unrelated to the offer (e.g., sports content with a finance offer); requires substantive topic overlap between content and offer

examples.text: A life coach trainer with 250k Instagram followers had built her following entirely through relationship content — red flags, green flags, men and women fighting in comments — and generated $3M annually from certification programs for aspiring life coaches. Despite large audience size, she reported zero crossover between followers and certification buyers. Alex Hormozi diagnosed this as having baited the hook with the wrong bait: her content perfectly marketed for life-coaching end-consumers (relationship advice seekers) rather than for aspiring life coaches who would buy her certification programs.

Alex Hormozi gave the concrete reframing instruction for the life coach trainer: instead of opening a video with a personal trauma or relationship lesson (e.g., 'here is how to deal with a partner who does X'), open with a client case framing: 'So she slapped her husband and I was like, oh my God, what's going on? This was a client that we had and this is the situation.' The same hook, the same story, the same expertise — but framed as a practitioner handling a client case rather than as a person experiencing a personal relationship problem. This single frame shift targets aspiring life coaches (who see themselves as the future practitioner) instead of end-consumers (who see themselves in the client's shoes).

Alex Hormozi cited a friend who ran a $50M life-coaching business by talking heavily about money as a source of life problems alongside her certification programs. This unified theme — money — naturally attracted both end-consumers (people whose life problems included money) and aspiring life coaches (who wanted to help people with those problems), enabling a two-tiered business structure where life coaching clients and certification buyers were reachable through a single content stream rather than requiring two separate content strategies.

practices.text: When pivoting content to attract practitioners rather than end-consumers, lead videos with client case-study hooks ('here is a client who came to me with this issue') rather than personal experience hooks ('here is what I experienced'), preserving all existing story and expertise while shifting which audience self-identifies with the content.

If you teach frameworks and philosophies inside a paid training, make those frameworks and philosophies the primary substance of free public content; do not withhold them out of fear of cannibalization.

Before rebuilding a content strategy from scratch, diagnose whether the problem is topic mismatch (requires new content) or frame mismatch (requires only repackaging existing content); frame mismatch requires only a wrapper change, not a content overhaul.

For a hotline-style interactive content format in a coaching business, have aspiring coaches or existing practitioners call in to discuss client situations rather than having end-consumers call in to discuss personal problems; this filters callers to match the offer audience.
